risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list that was followed by Delayed PE at five months (for full description see [9], [13])." Comment: The article states that participants were "randomized," but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated list, random number table, block randomization). Referring to prior publications without giving the method here leaves the sequence-generation process insufficiently described, so this domain is unclear rather than high risk.

Summary: The paper states that participants "were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list" and that "Participants provided oral consent to be interviewed by telephone and written informed consent for clinical assessments, randomization, and treatment." Comment: These statements confirm randomization and blinding for the SSRI/placebo arm, but they do not describe how the allocation sequence was concealed from the person enrolling participants (e.g. central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). No explicit concealment mechanism is reported, so allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list..." Comment: For the pharmacological comparison relevant to this review (SSRI vs placebo), the paper explicitly states the condition was double-blinded, indicating participants and personnel delivering the medication were blinded. Although PTSD outcomes are subjective and could be influenced by lack of blinding, the relevant drug/placebo comparison appears adequately blinded, so performance bias is judged low risk.

Quote: "The self-administered version of the PSS (PSS-SR; [36]) was used during clinical assessments." "The Kessler-6 (K6) is a brief 6-item self-report instrument... It was administered during telephone interviews." "Participants of the first clinical assessment (n = 756) were re-evaluated five months after the traumatic event (n = 604) regardless of treatment participation." "The final sample for the current study was n = 957, with 125 receiving CBT..." "Telephone- and clinical interviewers were blind to subjects' participation in the embedded steps (i.e., attending clinical interviews for telephone interviewers and attending treatment for clinical interviewers)." Comment: Outcome assessors included patient self-report and clinician/interviewer assessment. Although clinical interviewers were reported blind to treatment participation, the study also measured subjective outcomes by patient self-report (PSS-SR, K6). For these outcomes, the patient is the assessor, and participants would know whether they received CBT versus no CBT/waiting list. Because at least one outcome was subjective and self-reported without participant blinding, knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement; by the worst-outcome rule this domain is High risk.

Quote: "Participants who accepted the invitation (n = 296) were randomized to Prolonged Exposure therapy (PE [29]–[31]), Cognitive Therapy (CT [32]), a double-blinded SSRI/placebo condition, and a waiting list..." and "We utilized individuals who had data available at ten days and at least two additional time points... The final sample for the current study was n = 957, with 125 receiving CBT..." Comment: The paper reports overall cohort follow-up and a selected analysis sample, but it does not report post-randomization per-arm numbers for the randomized SSRI/placebo comparison (randomized vs analysed at each outcome time point), nor reasons for missing outcome data by arm. Because the analysed outcome denominators for the randomized pharmacological groups cannot be determined from this article, attrition bias for the outcomes of interest cannot be judged confidently.
